verbatimSummary: Behavioural addictions (BAs) share phenomenological overlap and neuropsychological similarities with obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD); however, the relationship remains understudied.
This study reports the prevalence of BAs in patients with OCD and healthy controls (HCs). It compares clinical characteristics including impulsivity traits among OCD subjects with and without BAs.

N = 150 OCD patients and 131 HCs assessed with structured instruments for: Internet / Sex / Pornography / Mobile phone / Kleptomania / Compulsive buying / Pyromania / Gambling / Food / Exercise. Impulsivity: BIS-11.
OCD had significantly higher prevalence of at least one co-occurring BA than HC: OCD 70%, HC 58%, p < 0.036.
OCD had significantly higher prevalence of Internet addiction (29.3% vs 3.1%), pornography addiction (12.7% vs 0%), sex addiction (10% vs 1.5%).
Mobile phone and food addiction were not significantly different by diagnostic criteria; OCD had significantly higher symptom intensity on severity scores for those domains.
OCD with BAs vs without: significantly more male, lower age at onset and at assessment; higher BIS total, motor, attentional subscales. No difference in symptom profile or OCD comorbidities.
Conclusion: co-occurrence of BAs in OCD is common; OCD+BA subgroup shows specific demographics and higher impulsivity.

subsections.title: Conceptual background
points: BAs: inability to control intense urges → persistent repetitive behaviour despite negative impacts (Kardefelt-Winther et al., 2017).
Overlap with OCD: recurrent thoughts/images, repetitive behaviour; BAs often ego-syntonic (pleasure/gratification/relief) vs ego-dystonic compulsions in OCD.
Impulsivity, decision-making, reward dysfunction noted in OCD + gambling (Grassi et al., 2020); habitual vs affective motivations in OCD vs alcohol use (Ferreira et al., 2021).

subsections.title: Evidence gap
points: Relationship OCD–BA understudied; clinically meaningful view needs systematic multi-BA assessment, not only single-BA or clinical impression studies.

Brakoulias et al. (2020) 6916 OCD across 13 countries: 8.7% problematic internet, 6.8% compulsive sexual behaviour, 6.4% compulsive buying, 4.1% gambling — mostly clinical diagnosis, limited instruments, subset of BAs.
OCD/OCS elevated in internet, compulsive buying, pathological gambling, compulsive sexual behaviours in various studies (cited).
OCD often compulsive end of spectrum; subgroup may have higher impulsivity (Kashyap et al., 2012). BAs linked to impulsivity → examine BIS in OCD.
Compare OCD with vs without BA on clinical characteristics.

subsections.title: Aims and hypotheses (paper wording)
points: Examine prevalence of BAs in OCD vs HC.
Compare socio-demographics, clinical features, impulsivity, mean comorbidities: OCD with vs without BA.
Hypothesis: OCD+BA distinctive clinically vs OCD without BA.
Hypothesis: impulsivity higher in OCD+BA vs OCD alone vs HC.

2.1_participants.setting: Specialty OCD clinic, NIMHANS, Bengaluru, India; new registrations Sep 2017–Nov 2018; clinic day Tuesday.
2.1_participants.inclusionOCD: Adults 18–60; primary diagnosis OCD; newly registered at clinic.
2.1_participants.exclusionOCD: Comorbid bipolar, psychotic disorders, substance dependence (except nicotine), organic brain disorders, intellectual developmental disorder, pervasive developmental disorders.
2.1_participants.recruitmentOCD: 320 new registrations invited; 38 not eligible; 112 refused; 150 completed (response rate 57.4%).
2.1_participants.assessor: First author; MD thesis; assessment duration ~1–1.5 h.
2.1_participants.healthyControls: Convenience sampling from community; matched for gender, age, years of education.
2.1_participants.exclusionHC: No lifetime Axis-I DSM-5 disorder (except nicotine dependence), no organic brain disorder, IDD, or PDD.

clinicalRoutine: Structured OCD clinic proforma by PG trainees: clinical details, Y-BOCS (Goodman et al., 1989), Axis-I comorbidities MINI-6.
All OCD patients on SSRIs at recommended dose range (Reddy et al., 2017).

studySpecific.instrument: BIS-11 (Patton et al. cited as JH et al., 1995 in PDF)
studySpecific.notes: 30 items; attentional, motor, planning factors; self-report; no ADHD diagnosis made.

studySpecific.scoring: 20 items, Likert 0–5; <30 normal; 31–49 mild; 50–79 moderate; 80–100 severe.

studySpecific.notes: 2 items (second with 6 sub-questions); Likert 1–5; cut-off from scoring equation < -1.34.

studySpecific.cutoff: >11 serious concern; 11 items Likert 0–4 last week.
Dependent if ≥3 DSM-IV substance dependence criteria met; Likert 1–6 past 3 months.

2.3_statistical_analysis.prevalence: Lifetime prevalence of BA calculated for OCD and HC.
2.3_statistical_analysis.severityComparisonOCDvsHC: Mann–Whitney for BA severity scores (non-normal distributions). Very low-prevalence BAs excluded from this analysis.
2.3_statistical_analysis.groupingOCD: OCD with BA = crossed cut-off on any BA scale; without = none.
2.3_statistical_analysis.OCDsubgroupComparisons: Chi-square and independent t-tests as appropriate for clinical profile, comorbidity, severity, impulsivity (including BIS subscales).
2.3_statistical_analysis.threeGroupBIS: ANOVA for BIS-11 total: HC vs OCD vs OCD with BA.
2.3_statistical_analysis.correlations: Whole sample: impulsivity vs each BA severity (continuous), excluding low-prevalence BAs.

2.4_ethics.approval: Ethical Committee, Department of Psychiatry, NIMHANS, Bengaluru.
2.4_ethics.dutyOfCare: Participants screening positive were psychoeducated and offered appropriate help.

narrativeNumbers: 150 OCD, 131 HC completed assessments; demographics comparable (Table 1).
Internet, pornography, sex prevalence differences Table 2.
Intensity (Mann–Whitney): internet, mobile, pornography, sex, food higher in OCD; compulsive buying p = 0.055 (Table 3).

OCD+BA vs OCD−BA: male 71.4% vs 51.1%; age onset 19.90 vs 26.69; age at assessment 28.41 vs 35.62; BIS total 73.73 vs 66.80; motor 23.08 vs 18.80; attentional 22.58 vs 19.96; planning NS; Y-BOCS, duration, family hx, comorbidity patterns NS (Table 4).
BIS total ANOVA three groups: HC 55.84±12.80, OCD 66.80±10.25, OCD+BA 73.73±12.27, p < 0.001 (Fig. 1).
Correlations: positive with internet, mobile, porn, sex, food BA scores; negative with compulsive buying (coefficients not in extracted text).

themes: Structured multi-BA assessment: internet, porn, sex higher in OCD vs HC; mobile & food higher intensity though not always categorical difference; compulsive buying not different; rare: exercise, kleptomania, pyromania.
First comparison with HC matched age/sex/education (authors' claim).
Did not replicate higher gambling/compulsive buying in some prior work; prior NIMHANS study also low gambling/CB (Jaisoorya et al., 2003).
Conceptual/criteria limitations for BAs; internet overlap with OCD phenomenology — authors ensured internet cases involved urges/gratification not typical OCD distress.
OCD+BA: male, younger onset/assessment; not different on illness duration, severity, family hx, comorbidity rates — discussion of whether demographic risk is universal vs OCD-specific.
Impulsivity: higher in OCD than HC; higher in OCD+BA than OCD-only; motor & attentional BIS elevated in OCD+BA vs Abramovitch cognitive-impulsivity discussion.
Correlation pattern links impulsivity to most BA scores except negative with compulsive buying.
Clinical implication: screen BAs in OCD; poorer outcomes with comorbidity cited (Fineberg et al., 2013).
Limitations: cut-offs = risk not necessarily diagnosis; treatment-seeking sample; cross-sectional; nicotine not analyzed; sensitive topics → under-reporting possible.

conclusion.text: Co-occurrence of BAs in OCD not uncommon. OCD+BA more likely male, lower age at onset, lower age at assessment, higher impulsivity. Clinicians should explore BAs in OCD given rising prevalence.
